Continuing through the fulfillment module, I'm mapping out the value objects for tracking and delivery logistics, along with the service layer that handles carrier integrations. Moving into billing, I'm documenting the invoice aggregates and payment structures that tie into the order lifecycle. Now I'm mapping out the import paths for each file—Money needs Currency from the shared enums, Product pulls in Id and Money from shared plus ProductState from catalog, and I need to trace the relative paths carefully from each file's location back to its dependencies. I'm continuing through the billing and notifications modules, mapping out the import paths for Payment, Refund, TaxLine, and other value objects that depend on shared Money types and their respective domain enums.

Now I'm looking at the service layer across multiple domains—ProductService, InventoryService, and PricingEngine all pull Product and Id from their respective paths, while CustomerService and AddressService follow the same pattern with Customer aggregates, and OrderService does the same with Order, with CartService following suit. Looking at the billing services, they follow the same pattern with Invoice aggregates and shared Id imports, then notifications services use EmailTemplate value-objects. Now I'm mapping out the orchestrators, starting with CheckoutOrchestrator which coordinates across customers, orders, and cart domains. I'm finishing up the import paths for the FulfillmentOrchestrator, then planning to write all the files in batches organized by module—starting with shared types, then module-specific types, services, and orchestrators in parallel. I need to be careful with TypeScript utility types like `Partial<Product>` which don't need imports, but I should verify what else needs to be imported for the service layer. Now I'm setting up the aggregate classes with their proper imports — `Product` will pull `Id` and `Money` from the shared module along with its local `ProductState` enum, while `Order` will similarly import `Id` from shared and reference its local `OrderStatus` and `OrderTotal`.
